title: WiiConnect24
requirements: A Wii with the Homebrew Channel installed
An SD card, any size up to 32GB
A Wii Remote and sensor bar
An internet connection on the Wii
A computer to download files and set up WiiLink
warnings: !!vWii (Wii U) users!! need to enable the 'Block IOS Reload' hack in Priiloader before installing WiiLink — otherwise you risk a brick.
RTC drift causes the Wii clock to desync over time. Fix it with sntp before connecting to WiiLink.
_note: WiiConnect24 was Nintendo's always-on connection service for the Wii, providing weather, news, message exchanges, Mii contests, and automatic updates. It was discontinued in 2013. WiiLink is a third-party replacement that restores these services.

steps.title: Before you start
steps.body:
This guide restores **WiiConnect24** functionality using **WiiLink**, a third-party service that brings back the weather, news, Nintendo Channel, Mii Contest Channel, and more.

You also fix the Wii's RTC (real-time clock) drift with **sntp**, so the console keeps accurate time — Wii services depend on it.

You need the **Homebrew Channel** installed first. If you haven't done that, follow the str2hax or another exploit guide first.

!!vWii (Wii U) users!! — before doing anything, open Priiloader and enable **Block IOS Reload** in System Menu Hacks. This prevents a brick when WiiLink patches IOS.

steps.title: Download sntp to your SD card
steps.body:
sntp is a tiny app that syncs the Wii's RTC clock over the internet so you don't have to manually set the date every time the battery dies.

Press the button and Graphite downloads sntp and sets it up in the right `apps` folder on your SD card.

steps.title: Clean up and eject the card
steps.body:
macOS scatters hidden junk files (`.DS_Store`, `._` files) onto SD cards, and Wii homebrew can choke on them.

Press the button, and Graphite strips those files off the card and safely ejects it for you. Once it pops off, pull the card out.

steps.title: Move the SD card to your Wii
steps.body:
Take the SD card out of your computer and slot it into the **SD card slot on the front of the Wii**. A USB adapter won't work — it must be the built-in slot.

Press **I did this** once the card is in.

steps.title: Launch sntp and sync the clock
steps.body:
With the SD card in the Wii, power it on and open the **Homebrew Channel**.

1. Launch **sntp** from the list of apps.
2. Use the D-pad to select your **timezone**.
3. Press the **A button** to sync. The app connects to a time server and updates the Wii's internal clock.
4. Press **HOME** or **B** to exit back to the Homebrew Channel.

Your RTC is now synced. The clock stays accurate as long as the internal battery holds — if it dies again, just run sntp again.

steps.title: Set up WiiLink on your Wii
steps.body:
WiiLink is a full replacement service that brings back the Weather Channel, News Channel, Everybody Votes Channel, Mii Contest Channel, Nintendo Channel, and more. Each channel requires its own patch.

On your **computer**, go to **https://wiilink.ca** and follow their guide:

1. Select the channels you want to restore.
2. WiiLink generates custom WAD files for your Wii's region.
3. You need a **WAD manager** (like YAWMM or Wii Mod Lite) installed on your Wii via the Homebrew Channel.
4. Copy the WAD files to your SD card and install each one through the WAD manager.

!!Important notes!!:
- WiiLink replaces official IOS slots — this is safe on real Wiis but requires **Block IOS Reload** on vWii.
- The channels show up as normal channels on the Wii Menu once installed.
- To use Mail/Voting/etc., you need a **WiiLink account** — sign up on their site.

This step is intentionally manual: WiiLink's setup changes over time, and their own guide is always up to date.

steps.title: You're done
steps.body:
Your Wii now has working **WiiConnect24** services through **WiiLink**. The channels appear on your Wii Menu just like the originals.

If the clock drifts again later, you can always re-run **sntp** from the Homebrew Channel.

Check back at wiilink.ca for new channels and updates.
